title: A color engine built for the brand, not the industry default
subtitle: Perceptual color extraction from content artwork — dynamic theming that stays warm, meets WCAG contrast, and works in light and dark mode
team: Design-led, with an engineering partner. Hamburg / Berlin

hero_image.alt: The Color Lab interface: configuration sliders on the left (light- and dark-mode normalization curves, hue-exclusion gradients) and two phone previews of the same Bibel TV content card on the right — one in dark mode, one in light mode — both themed from the same extracted palette

sections.headline: A color engine built for the brand, not the industry default
sections.subtext: Every streaming app pulls colors from its artwork. Most work in RGB and assume dark mode. I built an extraction engine in OKLCH, the perceptual color space, so Bibel TV gets warm theming that holds up in light and dark mode and always meets WCAG contrast.

sections.quote: You cannot just invert dark-mode logic. Extracting color that works in light mode is a different problem.
sections.context: Why an off-the-shelf library would not do

sections.headline: What the engine does, and why standard tools don't
sections.content:
From a single piece of content artwork, the API returns a full theme: a base color, an accent color, one or two text colors, and a page-background gradient. The base color anchors the card gradient that sits over the artwork. The accent carries the logo. The text colors are guaranteed to clear AA or AAA contrast against the base — no manual checking.

The algorithm is short. K-means clustering finds the most prominent colors in the artwork; a hue-and-vibrance filter then drops the clusters that are dull or neutral, so dull greys never win just by covering the most pixels. The remaining clusters are mapped to roles — base, accent, text. Text colors are validated against the base using the WCAG contrast algorithm; if the pair fails, lightness and chroma are adjusted until it passes. The page-background gradient is computed from the base color at reduced opacity, so the page feels like it belongs to the content.

All of that happens in OKLCH, not RGB. Most extraction tools work in RGB and tune for contrast against dark backgrounds. Flip to light mode and the same colors wash out the text or turn harsh. OKLCH is perceptually uniform: you can reason about lightness and chroma directly, which is what makes precise contrast and a natural gradient blend possible in both modes.

Designers tune the engine through Color Lab — the internal configuration tool shown above. The normalization curves and hue-exclusion gradients on the left of that screen are how a designer adjusts the extraction parameters per brand or per artwork type; the two phone previews on the right show the same theme rendered in dark and light mode.

steps.label: Load the artwork
steps.description: Content image loaded from a URL or upload, converted to a pixel array.

steps.label: Cluster in OKLCH
steps.description: K-means finds the most prominent colors, working in OKLCH so proximity matches human vision, not just math.

steps.label: Filter for character
steps.description: Low-vibrance clusters are dropped, so the theme is built from colors that actually define the artwork.

steps.label: Map to roles
steps.description: Clusters are assigned to base, accent, and text roles, and the page-background gradient is computed from the base color.

steps.label: Validate contrast
steps.description: Text colors are checked against the base with the WCAG algorithm. Lightness and chroma are adjusted until the pair passes.

steps.label: Return the theme
steps.description: A JSON theme — colors in hex and OKLCH, gradient config, contrast ratios — ready to use directly in app theming.

sections.headline: The decisions that mattered
sections.content:
**Design owned the infrastructure.** Color extraction usually lives with platform engineering. I owned it, because the choices inside it — hue thresholds, vibrance filters, perceptual versus mathematical proximity — are design decisions. Delegate them to a generic API and you delegate brand quality.

**Light mode came first.** Bibel TV's brand is warm, hopeful, light. Users associate it with brightness, not a cinema black-out. So the pipeline was tuned for light-mode correctness first, then verified in dark mode — the reverse of the streaming-app norm.

**Restraint on the phone.** Bibel TV's 2024 on-air rebrand uses two text colors for visual hierarchy. The engine produces both. On a dense phone display they read as noise, so mobile ships with one. The engine keeps the capability; the design team decides when to use it.

**The app should feel like the broadcast.** The card gradient and the page gradient are digital translations of the on-air color language. Every piece of artwork produces a theme that feels like Bibel TV, not like Netflix with a different logo — and it happens automatically, with no manual color matching.

metrics.label: Light mode preference
metrics.value: 87% of validation-survey users preferred light mode (n=40)

metrics.label: Contrast compliance
metrics.value: Every extracted theme validated at AA or AAA — no manual checking

metrics.label: Delivery
metrics.value: API live, config UI in use, integrated with the design team's Figma plugin

sections.headline: What I took from it
sections.content:
Infrastructure work is design work. The thresholds and filters inside the extraction algorithm are design decisions, and when you hand them to a generic library you hand over brand quality with them.

The other lesson was restraint. The engine can produce complex multi-tone looks. On a phone, the simpler output was the better one. Sometimes the design decision is to not use what you built.

executive.summary: Built a K-means color extraction engine in OKLCH that powers dynamic theming, card gradients, and WCAG-compliant text for the Bibel TV app. 87% light-mode preference, zero manual contrast checking, brand-consistent by default.
executive.angle: Design-led infrastructure, when industry defaults don't fit the brand

blog.summary: Every streaming app defaults to dark mode. Bibel TV had to make theming work in light, for users who want warmth. Here is how the color engine does it.
blog.angle: The technical depth behind a color problem the industry skips

meta.source: bibel-tv experience file + Bibel TV project interview 2026-05-06 + Figma plugin source + Jupus case-study quality pass 2026-05-22
